36394:
B.

Extracts from the Secretary of War's letters to the late Major General Greene.

July 10<sup>th</sup> 1782.

"The sufferings of your troops have impressed me with the deepest concern, and the very painful sensations, which your relation of them excites, are powerfully enhanced, that these distresses should have been the lot of an army, not only intitled, by special contract, to better fare, but whose meritorious and gallant exertions under the most perilous [difficulties]
36395:
difficulties, merited a very different fate; mine is the unhappy station, in which I must hear complaints, without having it in my power to redress the grievances."

"September 30<sup>th</sup> 1782

"The moment you have taken your determinations, what hoops you will retain, I wish to be informed, whether they can, or cannot be supplied with clothing, with you: if this, I suppose, there will be no doubt, in case Charleston should to be evacuated — indeed, we had almost better give any price, than think of sending it from here. We have met with so many losses and delays, that we have little hope of success, should it be again attempted, however, if the clothing cannot be had with you, it must go from hence."

"7 November 5<sup>th</sup> 1782.

"If the whole (the army) are to remain, and Charleston is not left by the British, large supplies must be immediately forwarded — if it should be evacuated, I hope, we shall have it in our power to procure the necessary articles of clothing in that town. On these matters, I wish for the earliest information."

"December 1<sup>st</sup> 1782.

"I trust, you will be enabled to furnish an ample supply of clothing for the hoops, from the Warehouses in Charleston, as I mentioned to you in a former letter."

"If clothing cannot be supplied there, I hope, we shall be in a capacity to afford you a considerable supply from Virginia, which I [undecipherable]

36397:
C.

Extract of a letter from Major General Greene, to Major General Lincoln, Secretary at War, dated

"Head Quarters, November 6<sup>th</sup> 1782."

"I am taking measures to obtain clothing for the troops. We have on hand but a small part of our winter clothing, and after what we shall be obliged to spare to those troops going northwardly, we shall have but a small pittance left. I imagine our purchases will amount to not less than forty thousand dollars, for which I shall draw bills on the Finances; and, as I provide the clothing at your instance, and by your order, I hope, you will prepare the Finances for the draughts, that the bills may be punctually paid. I have already drawn in favor of M<sup>r</sup> Banks and Company, for eight thousand dollars, in bills of different values, to secure the clothing, and by this step, I am in hopes to save twenty per cent on the goods — I aim to advance twelve hundred guineas, which, I am in hopes to get from M<sup>r</sup> Hall, the Continental Receiver — You will please to inform M<sup>r</sup> Morris, that I have applied, and propose to appropriate this sum to the payment of the clothing. If, in any thing, I have exceeded your intentions, you will please to inform me. My estimates are barely sufficient for covering the troops, and, as I am informed, the northern army is completely clad, and, as you mention a desire that this should, I have laid out accordingly, as far as the articles necessary for the purpose, could be had; many things cannot."

36404:
Stratford Feb<sup>y</sup> 5<sup>th</sup> 1793

Dear Sir

If you recollect when I was last at your Office you returned me the inclosed account of Dan Barton Nation which was returned from the War Office with the note made on the Back, since which I have obtained the Certificate required as you may see made under P. Account —

I will Thank you to forward the Amount which is 17 Doll<sup>s</sup> & I Pray M<sup>r</sup> Smith as it will be difficult for me to find an opportunity to send for it again — but if it is convenient you would pay me in Morriss or Milligan's — Notes, as the Sum is but small —

I have also forwarded by M<sup>r</sup> Smith my Accounts of Pay for the last 3 Years Service, the Accounts for the different Months have not made out, but have sent Blank Papers Signed every Provo for P. Accounts to be made up and a brief to be made upon the blank, agreeable to your direction. These accounts should have been sent by M<sup>r</sup> Parks but he went from S. Harvin sooner than I expected — and at present it is inconvenient for me to attend, which I hope will excuse my

36424:
Philad<sup>a</sup> January 3<sup>d</sup> 1794

The Iron Masters to whom great James are due, are frequently applying to me to be informed whether the Nails & Shot at their several Furnaces belonging to the public, are to be sold — As you are fully acquainted with the nature of their demands, the ability of the States in any other way than that they propose to discharge them, and can also form the best judgement relative to the propriety of disposing of such part as may be equal to their claims; if not the whole on hand; I have thought fit to place you opinions previous to returning an Answer on the Subject — The Store by the late act of Congress being wholly under my direction, I conceive I am Authorised & shall be fully justified in disposing of such as may not be considered as especially necessary to compose the Magazines intended to be kept in the Peace establishment provided it can be done with evident advantage — In the present case the Bills of the Iron Masters taken conected will inform you that the Nails on an average cost about L<sup>d</sup> 1<sup>s</sup> — 4 Ton — the Shot about L<sup>d</sup> 8<sup>s</sup> — a Ton — The former will now command from ten to twelve pounds p<sup>r</sup> ton, and the latter from Seven to Nine pounds p<sup>r</sup> ton, with this State of the matter in view it only remains to determine whether it is best to let the balances of the Accounts in question continue to accumulate the demands, or sell as many Shot & Nails as will pay them off immediately — being clearly of opinion myself that the latter is the most eligible. I thought it my duty to Submit the matter to your consideration — shall only add that as Bar Iron is in great demand & consequently high, the present seems the most likely time to sell the Shot & Nails advantageously as they will easily be drawn into Bars —

S H

Hon. Sup<sup>t</sup> Finance

36435:
Philadelphia January 6 1800

Sir

M<sup>r</sup> Louis is desirous to be informed whether there is a prospect of your continuing for any length of time in your present Situation, as private Arrangements may depend on the information requested. I will candidly say all I know about the matter. But you may take such measures as the interest of your family may dictate — As yet no establishment either in Troops or Stores is agreed on — The Idea of Congress as far as I can learn is to have five federal Depots for Military Stores in the States, but when or how they will be able to procure Suitable buildings I know not — or how the charge will eventually be disposed of — a New establishment will of necessity abridge both the number of Officers & the Salaries allowed them. Thus a living solely from the employment cannot be hoped for the present you will be continued, at least until I receive further Orders, for your services have been acceptable to me — and when the final arrangement is made of it I have any hand in the Appointment, with any thing offer worthy your acceptance you will have the preference — this is all I can say in the Subject — perhaps it may be as well to prepare your Accounts and returns up to the 1<sup>st</sup> Jn<sup>y</sup> and pay us a visit when we may converse more intelligibly —

[strikethrough: Captain Emis]

T H

Captain Emis
36436:
Philad<sup>a</sup> January 6 1784

Sir

Your letter mentioning the horse on hand, (by the Stage) is not come to hand, the Duplicate of the 1<sup>st</sup> Instant I have received — As he is valuable I think with you, that he had better be disposed of in this City, for which purpose you will send him down as soon as possible — I wish to receive the Accounts very soon for the reasons I gave you in my last letter — Am happy to find your State is taking measures with Virginia relative to land encroachments. I have long thought something ought to be done — the Appointment will compass what they have in View — My Compliments to M<sup>rs</sup> Morgan —

S H

Colonel Morgan

36441:
[P.S.] [①]

Philadelphia Jan 71, 1784.

I have just time to inform you that a thaw has so broken the ice as to enable Starbuck to slip out this morning. Before one o'Clock he was round Gloucester Point, and I trust he will safely get to sea. Swift his safe arrival I have by a former post advised you that I had put on board him a rolling screen, a shorts cloth of trap wire, and two riddles, for your mill. — His sailing earlier than I expected to day prevented my getting bills of lading signed. In haste

Tim. Pickering

Benj<sup>a</sup> Lincoln Esq<sup>r</sup>.

36447:
New York Jan<sup>s</sup> 10<sup>th</sup> 1784

Dear Sir

Your Favor of the 6 Instant I have received, in obediance to the Acknewledge the receipt of the Sunday Packets I forwarded last week by the Albany Packet to be left at Major Campbell on the Village at [undecipherable] place I Judge it will be most convenient to the Port & the Gent. at West point to have their Letters left for them but I wish to have ourselves, or "Officers acquainted that as I... letters and Letters now in the office, to find a great many more may daily be brought charged with postage, that as I am Answerable for such, perhaps I trust they could fall on some method to have them forwarded & the postage paid. I suppose Major Campbell, would take that on him to oblige the Officers. I could then send the amount of such Letters, which he could pay to my Postanders.

I have been very unwell for several days, with a just added to a confirmed billiny vomit, but am now much better —

In regard to what you mention respecting some Cash. I think it my duty for your exceeding kindness and politeness extended me when in great want, to exert every resource to comply with your request. I have therefore this day borrowed from a friend [undecipherable] I being unable to go out, and it is due

[32P11]
36448:
Newburgh Jan<sup>y</sup> 18<sup>th</sup> 84

[Sir]

You will doubtless recollect, that when I reported on you in the evening of the 11<sup>th</sup> Ult<sup>o</sup> respecting my pay as Post Master to the [Army], you were pleased in consequence of the Parace Salary, & being the only person of that department, to promise me the payment of it without sending the Accounts to Philad<sup>a</sup> for a Warrant, conceiving that you would be sufficiently justified in doing it.

M<sup>r</sup> Wolfe who lately presented the account for payment, informs me that you could not then pay it & that you did not know whether you could do it at all; in this case I must beg of you to inform me what method I shall take to obtain it; and for I shall find it very hard to be put off with a Certificate, after having so fair a promise of pay in Bills at least, if not Cash — If a request or Order from the Post Master Gen<sup>l</sup> will be sufficient, I will thank

36454:
Philad.<sup>a</sup> January 12, 1784

Upon receipt of this, you will proceed to French Creek where is a Magazine belonging to the public; your business when there received is with the utmost attention to [undecipherable: superintend] it, and thereby prevent loss either by accident or design, for this purpose you will obtain quarters as contiguous to the Magazine as possible, and every morning, noon and evening you will go to it, and know that it is in safety — for this service your wages shall be eight Spanish Mill'd Dollars per Month — as often as necessary you will inform me, the State of your charge, especially if any attempt by breaking in or otherwise is discovered — you shall promptly receive your pay as it becomes due —

S. H.

M<sup>r</sup>. Clark —
36456:
[Samuel Hodgdon Regt. A L M?]
Newburgh Jan<sup>y</sup> 12. 1794

D<sup>r</sup> Sir,

Some of the waggoners who done the Carriage of General Washingtons to his estate have return'd to him. It seems they drew clothing from Philadelphia, for which, they were accountable on their return, but they have not done it, nor have I any account of the clothing from here, which has come to some difficulty on Settling with them. Be so good as to find that account and to inform whether any and what money they have received of you or for Brig general, directing your letter to David Wolfe A.Q.M. at this place.

M<sup>r</sup> Quackenbush, the quarter master at Albany, has for the public buildings and timber &c there are in his district towards the payment of the Public debts of 1792 & 1793. It is necessary that he should complete the Settlements with and his creditors entirely, where he has mostly partial payments. the Suppose about five thousand dollars will do this, but he is to send to my office the most exact account he can make, which when examined is to be transmitted to you to endeavour to obtain tickets to the amount. I was favored with yours of the 4<sup>th</sup> Instant and with Pleasure comply with your request and enclose you a list of Such of the Gentlemen of the Hospital Department as are entitled to the Commutation of five Years full Pay, who have been arranged to come under the Resolve of Congress of the 17<sup>th</sup> January 1781.

36468:
Philadelphia Jan<sup>y</sup> 16. 1793

Sir,

I take the liberty to enclose the accounts I mentioned to you in a former Letter, you will please to observe they are made up to the 1<sup>st</sup> January 1793 — I also enclose a number of Certificates for the purpose of receiving a Warrant for the sums that they were given by the Loan Masters for Advances received, which are to be deducted from their respective balances, agreeable to the rules of account, which will in some instances render the account as Liquidated, a circumstance that on final Settlement must be duly attended to. I am not certain that the receipts are given for certain all the Advances delivered, but as the returns have not been regularly made to the War Office by the Numerous Officers authorized to Negotiate the bounty, I mean to take a Declaration on Oath, or on honor as you dictate, from each of the Loan Masters, previous to Settlement, relative to the sums he received — have only to add, that on the way I had the honour to propose in my last, or in some other way more agreeable to you, I wish to be enabled to close this business, as I am so happy in having to carry the Sums paid to each in advance, forward in my quarterly Settlement —

S H

Honorable Suprt of Finance

Sir,                                        Office of Finance 19. Jan. 1784

I am to acknowledge the Favor of your Letter of the third Instant from West Point. On the twenty second of last Month, I replied to yours of the eighth and eighteenth the Contents of which Letters will have informed you that I had taken the Arrangements which you desire for Supply of the Troops. I have also determined to enable the Paymaster (if possible) to pay the Officers their Subsista-

[377] nce at the Commencement of every Month. I send you have a Copy of my Letter to Gen.<sup>t</sup> Jackson agreeable your Request. the Contents of it will I hope meet your Wishes.

With perfect Esteem and Respect.

I have the Honor to be Sir
your most obedient

36489:
Sir,

Philad<sup>a</sup> January 23. 1784

To day I received your favour of the 2<sup>d</sup> Instant, inclosing one for Colonel Pickering, and another for Captain Peochike — both are absent, the first is gone to Boston, the latta to Annapolis, yet no time shall be lost in the delivery — inclosed is a letter from M<sup>r</sup> Frotthingham, who is also at Boston, if any communication is necessary in consequence of it, by addressing to me, your letters will get speedily and safely to hand —

S. Ho—

Colonel Carrington —
36490:
Philad<sup>a</sup> January 23. 1784

Sir

It is so long since I have heard from you, I am at a loss where to address to you; upon receipt of this I wish you to make out, and as soon after as poss-ible transmit to me a very Accurate State of the Post, under your direction in which include as near as you can, an estimate of all sums due from the Public for which you stand par-ticularly engaged — and a return of every Species of Stores, noting the place of deposit of each and their present State — and as soon as convenient, I wish to receive your Accounts for expenditures — Let me hear from you in return to this —

J.H.

Captain Trish—

S H

Hon. Sup.<sup>t</sup> of Finance

Boston Feb<sup>y</sup> 5 1784 —

Dear Sir

I wrote to you some time Since, respecting a bill drawn by Some merchants in Charleston S<sup>o</sup> Carolina on Mr. [strikethrough: Petit] Petit which bill I left in the hands of Mr — Carleton I wrote you would know whether Mr. Petit will or will not pay it — If he will not pay it I wrote you would write to Mr. Ferguson of S<sup>o</sup> Carolina on the Subject

36535:
[Letter from General Knox dated Feb<sup>y</sup> 1784 relative to General Green's Ordnance]

Boston 7 February 1784

Dear Sir —

Some time past I am informed that the Commander in Chief should present two cannon to Major General Greene which had been taken at Pulan or some other place to the Southward — but it is difficult if not impossible in any reasonable time to procure those Cannon and have [strikethrough: the] them engraved upon them the substance of the resolve of Congress. In consideration of this difficulty His Excellency General Washington decided that Some of the Cannon which were at West point Should be engraved for this purpose, but, it was frustrated by all the cannon at that post having been previously engraved — I have therefore to request that you would have two of the british cannon now in Philadelphia engraved with the enclosed inscription, — It will be necessary to have the words encircled by some handsome emblems.

I beg you would be so good as to put this matter in train immediately, and let me know directed to me at this place the probable time when they will be finished — The Carriages also must be put into good order. I am persuaded that the Honorable Mr. Financier will pay the expences arising upon this business upon your application to him, and Shewing him a Copy of this request.

If you Should have any doubts upon your mind respecting the propriety of complying with this request, you will please to apply to the War Office for further information, or direction.

I am dear Sir

your very humble
Servant
H Knox M<sup>Gen</sup>

Samuel Hodgdon Esquire

36575:
Sir

Philad<sup>a</sup> Feb<sup>ry</sup> 16. 1784

By a Letter from M<sup>r</sup> Beng<sup>n</sup> Huntingdon dated New Haven 4<sup>th</sup> instant address'd to the Superintendant Denance, it appears there are a considerable number or quantity of Military Stores lying without regard on several places in the State of Connecticut, particularly in Hartford, upon receipt of this I have to request that you immediately make diligent enquiry for all such Stores & being found that you take measures to have them removed to Springfield & deposited under your immediate direction in common with those already at that depository, perhaps you may find it necessary to call on M<sup>r</sup> Huntingdon for a particular enumeration of the places where they may be found, as several are alluded to in his Letter, if a personal application is necessary you will make it, the expences of which, with those attending any Stores that may be found shall be paid on receipt of the Accounts — have only to request, that in future by every possible means you will endeavour to prevent any farther applications of this kind in the Vicinity of your charge, as they are disreputable to the Dep<sup>t</sup> at large — Make me a return of any that may be received in consequence of this [underline: Letter] —

S H

[Captain Int. Briant]

36585:
[Joe Carleton. Sec<sup>y</sup> of War]

Litton — June February 13. 1785

[Copy]

I have been honour'd with your Letter of the 16<sup>th</sup> December last, address'd to the late Secretary at War.

The resolution of Congress of the 30<sup>th</sup> September last, giving permission by Brevet, extends only to such Officers as were continued in Service after the 7<sup>th</sup> January 1783 — by comparing your returns with the last Arrangements of the North Carolina line, I find that Colonel Harke, L<sup>t</sup> Colonel Lytle, Major Hogg, Captains Tharfode, Hall and Sharp and Lieutenants Camper have a claim under that act — the Brevets for these Gentlemen as well as Harrishment Commissions ascertaining the former rank of the other Officers continued on your returns, shall be given and forward to you without delay.

I have the Honor to be

Sir Your most obed<sup>t</sup>
and very Hble Serv<sup>t</sup>

[H. Knox, Secretary]
[the Records of the War Department, by authority from Congress]

The Hon<sup>ble</sup>
Brigadier Gen<sup>l</sup> Sumner

Dear Sir,

In setting [strikethrough: accounts] with the Commissaries here, I find that I am charged with 47 [undecipherable] Dollars for — Dress Articles of Cloathing which have been [strikethrough: grants] to Dennis Harteke [?] of [undecipherable] by whom [undecipherable] that Deputy in St. Lardelin. I am therefor requested to ask that Deputy to a[ffi]dat in Relation to the Debt, also to obtain from [undecipherable] of any Subsistence Money or the Allowance for Servants Cloathing, to that attention Matter in a little — to furnish us and letting Affidavit upon my accounts due to settle here for which Claims. I have not kept any of the above Accounts, that the Deputy Paymaster has having calculated the amount of my Subsistence and compared it with Dates, after which I had a have letter me to be believed, have found, that there was not a Deduction charged against to the above — now written answer. I am requested to ask you for one of the Deputies here, however — on the Matter we may determine from the above account that Commissaries on my side in hereby relying on my Merits. I trust that my Pay would be little without that necessary Deduction from what I would be much obliged to you.

I hope for the best, intend to return to Europe — I hope to see you before that time, to receive your Compliments from you on any of these Fields. I am with great Respect

Sir

Philadelphia February 21. 1789

Yours of the 11<sup>th</sup> Dec<sup>r</sup> came to hand by Post yesterday — M<sup>r</sup> Morris has never mentioned to me any thing of the three thousand Dollars intended for you — but I will now apply to him, as I conclude on the Multiplicity of business it has escaped his Memory. — and when obtained you may rest assured not a moments time shall be lost in getting it to you — Colonel Pickering is present; he intends once more to visit the Remains of the Army and then close his accounts. —

J.P.

[9]

Dear Sir

Your favor of the 27 January and 26 Feb<sup>y</sup> came to hand the 18<sup>th</sup> Instant I thank you most gratefully for your attention to my Business. It gives me great Satisfaction to know that the Mill Stone of Maller may be had in time — if they are here in all June it will do — I cannot relinquish the idea of having the Stone four feet in Sight or nine inches diametre or near that Size for my mills will be destroyed at high water it becomes therefore necessary for me to have Stones which will be found dispatched when the tide suits, particularly those in front of the mills Lord as offsite [undecipherable] will be enough I can apply a force equal to the weight of the Stones — McCullum mfs.

Your own I Yours &c most directs you relative to the propriety of taking Stones made in Philadelphia of those made in [undecipherable] will have it in your power with the advice of people who are knowing the matter —

M<sup>c</sup>Cullen informs me that

'tis probable the bill on M<sup>r</sup> Pettit will be paid as soon as the House shall open at any rate I have no doubt but this bill or another from M<sup>r</sup> Furger you will from place money in your hands to [undecipherable] pay for the Stones should they — I should that not be the case your Draft on me will be honored at [strikethrough: two] [undecipherable] fifths. +

I am D Sir with rence

Affectionately M<sup>r</sup> [undecipherable]

Your Ob<sup>t</sup> Servant

M<sup>r</sup> S Hodgdon

+ I wish you would call on the Clothier General for a Duplicate of the receipt of the Clothing I sold him in the beginning of 1782 the property of this State the one he gave me is worn out [undecipherable] — pray for word it by the first opportunity

Telling him Captain had — the Money in his hands and he promised me Some time ago that he would give you thirty dollars in part of what he has received on their accounts — if he has not done it, I beg you will see him and negotiate the matter before you have

the North River — I have no [undecipherable] way of getting this affair settled but thro you —

God bless you

J<sup>r</sup> Christie

36681:
Philad<sup>a</sup> 24<sup>th</sup> March 1781

Sir

The bearer M<sup>r</sup> Ingles is settling his accounts with the public, to do this Acceptably the stores must also be Accounted for — On reference to my books for receivals he finds a number of Barrels of Powder which he has not Entitled this it seems happened by his being in this City at the time the powder was sent to him at Lancaster, he now comes on to rectify the mistake, as there must have been an omission of these when M<sup>r</sup> Hall receipted to him, and probably when you received them of Hall, I wish you by counting what is now on hand & Adding any deliveries, would Ascertain whether what you received was right and in any other way Assist M<sup>r</sup> Ingles to find out and rectify his mistake —

S H.

William Henry Esquire —

Sir

Yours &c.

36689:
John Pierce Esq<sup>r</sup> Pay Master General
Newburgh March 26 1784

Sir,

I have received your favour of the 4<sup>th</sup> with the enclosure. Your forage account will be adjusted in the way you propose. among other demands of mine is one for 8 dollars subsistence of Lt<sup>t</sup> Ebenezer Brown for June last. Having gone by muster to Paterson's Brigade, and paid him his Subsistence for that month in cash, and since General Patterson's certificate, but when I am transmitting the accounts for the Subsistence of Francis detachment for that month, I find Lt Browne — thinking his Subsistence would be included in his account, as any detachment, but it was not. I have to imagine he could not afterwards receive the Subsistence of you for the same month. I beg as a very convenience to examine the accounts of his account, I will thank you to do it and to inform me with this, he received his Subsistence there for June last —

Colonel Varick is in New York, where his forage account is to be settled when I am in case.

I shall wish to move my effects to New York, falls with. When Judge Col. Palmer with the Scheme, had concluded his acquit the farming of posts also papers liable to damage from Water. And therefore determined to embark mine in some private vessel bound to New York. I will now engage one for both of us at the same time, but for this end wish for to give me some news of the bulk of your moveables, that I may provide room for them —

I am dear Sir,
with great regard
Your mo<sup>st</sup> obed<sup>t</sup> serv<sup>t</sup>

Timothy Pickering

36703:
Sir

Philad.<sup>a</sup> April 1<sup>st</sup> 1794 —

I have been favoured with yours of the 24<sup>th</sup> January. The Accounts you forward cannot be Settled with any other than the Principal in the department under whose immediate notice the Debts were contracted, as it is an indispensible rule, to have the Stores accounted for by every public Officer with his Cash Accounts; thus circumstanced I cannot meddle with them. In any case where you may have become immediately Accountable, by forwarding your Accounts, with the expenditures, or delivery of the Stores purchased & received to the Comptroller Office in this City, they will be liquidated independently, and Certificates for the balance due you will issue —

M<sup>r</sup> Bourne Price

36710:
[Hodgson
6 April 84]

Dear Genreal

May two or three last Letters remain un= =swered, I am fearful they have Miscarried for I know you attention — By your Friend of God you will receive this. In one of the letters alluded to I informed you that it was the general opinion here that the Dimensions given by you for the Mill Stones were unanimously large numerically and not Scarce, and, requested further consideration on the subject before I engaged for them. I can have either those made in England or those made here by the time you will need them, and nearly at a price. Friend Moreton says the long risks are best — I have had a conference with a Man who by recommendation and appearance Seems qualified to undertake both the erection and working of the Mills. he is a Single Man about fifty or [undecipherable]

Englishman and believes he was regularly bred to every part of the buisiness. I Saw him Making the Burr Stones and he appeared well acquainted with that part — but I am not Sanguine, I have Seen too many English Bits — however, as his terms are Moderate Viz<sup>t</sup> One Guinea per week, if nothing more certain Offers and you approve of it I will engage with him for two Months and Send him to you — If you looked him I am inclined to think a half Guinea per week may engage him; please to let me hear from you on the subject as Soon as convenient — I have heard nothing farther either from M<sup>r</sup> Carleton or Pettit relative to the Bills, I will take Such Measures with re= =pect to them as your Great may direct — I have repeatedly Mentioned Colonel Walch's matter to M<sup>r</sup> Monys, but as yet nothing is done, I hope there will

will Shortly, if So I will advise both you & him

Nothing particularly new, no appointment to the Office you resigned, nor is it likely it will Soon take place, General Knox is looked at as likely to Succeed to it — trade begins to flourish — By Cap<sup>t</sup> Starbuck in a Brig belonging to Nantucket I Shall Send you two Barrels of most excellent Superfine flour, and by Cap<sup>t</sup> Cole two Barrels of the most common — both will probably Sail for Boston in eight or ten days —

With affection and esteem
[left margin: Superfine flour
6 Dollars per Barrell
Common 5 ¼ ditto
Ann Currency 4$ 3/]
I am as ever
Sir
your Most Obedient Servant

Sam Hodgson

Genreal Lincoln —

Philadelphia August 2<sup>d</sup> 1784

No.                                                                                                              Letter 6 new

Since my return to this City, I have seen a young [undecipherable] of the 9<sup>th</sup> Ulto. I have applied to the War Office for the information you required concerning Brig Gen. Anderson and am not able to find where he entered this service or when he died— by the Resolves of Congress it appears he was made a Brig of Engineers on the 17<sup>th</sup> of November 1777— you will find by the inclosed Article of his account that he has received more than he received pay in the [undecipherable] transactions and that it is doubtful whether his is entitled to have his depreciation altho he not being [strikethrough: out of our minds and] comprehended in the promise of Congress of the 8<sup>th</sup> of April 1780 —
The resolutions of Aug. 10 1779 and August 10 1780 promising one years half pay to the Widows &
Officers dying in the service, does not appear to respect the Foreigners, the former is made solely for the Officers of the States and the latter is predicated on the resolution of May 15 1778 which seem to relate [strikethrough: there] to those engagees and also refers the resolving it up to the State to which such Officer belong, which may render it necessary that myself or more than appeared of Congress be further authorized, before [undecipherable] can be concluded, and
let you such claims can be admitted —

[Copy] —     West Point 21 April 1784

To

I received yours of 16<sup>th</sup> Instant respecting the powder Carbs, for my part I should think the Matter had better be deft till next fall if they Cannot be procured at most for four Shillings Currency and then if it should be determined to keep what Powder is here to make the Cartment. In the mean while to get laths and repair the old Carbs the Season, which if we have [strikethrough: gotten] a sufficient number of Cottons Can be done, though not so much to the purpifs. I shall be Glad of an Answer from you as soon as Convenient upon the Subject.       1/2362
I am Sir your most
Humb<sup>le</sup> Serv<sup>t</sup>
William Rice
B<sup>y</sup> M<sup>c</sup> Henry

Col Timothy Pickering
L. M. Gend
36739:
April 24 1784

Sir

In consequence of a letter received from Major Gibbs I enclose to your address fifteen pair of Silver Epauletts for the Officers at West Point. The annexed invoice ascertains the Value of each Sett, which you will please to receive and account with me for those Marked for General Jackson, Colonel Hall & Major Gibbs, you will be kind enough to deliver, the others you will deliver to such as call for them, observing the Grades —

The Captains receive one plain epaulet, and Strap or counterpart; the Lieutenants one of each Interspersed with Silk — If more are wanted, or Swords such as the Marquis presented to the Officers, please to inform me, and they shall be sent on ——  I am Sir.

Your most Obedient Servant
S. H.

[Captain Gondy]
36740:
Dear Sirs

April 24. 1784

I was duly favoured with yours of the 31 Ultimo, and agreeable to your request have forwarded fifteen pairs of Epaulets for the Officers of your Like, for which you and they will please to account with Captain Fondy to whom they are Address'd, at the prices inserted on his Invoice, that being Agreeable to what the Like kinds have always bought — I had but one pair left with Bullion & them are to be delivered General Jackson, the came for his grades you may easily have them added to yours if you choose — If Captain Fondy should not be at York you will please to open the package and Stock yours — They are particularly marked for you —

S.H.

Major Gibbs
36741:
[Major Gibbs —]

Philad<sup>a</sup> April 24 1784

Sir   I have been favoured with your Letter of the 20<sup>th</sup> Ins<sup>t</sup>— Col Eckhart previous to his leaving this City received Instructions from me relative to M<sup>r</sup> Youngkmans concern, he is to first to Sele the remaining half of the building, & then to determine as justice will warrant on M<sup>r</sup> Youngkmans purchase, M<sup>r</sup> Youngkmans Character stands fair with me, and he may rest assured he will be treated generously at the conclusion of the business — Should I come your way, I shall accept your polite Invitation, and pay my respects to you at your Dwelling —

[Cap<sup>t</sup> C. Biddle +]   S H
36742:
[346]

Philadelphia April 24 1784 —

Sir,

A certain M<sup>r</sup> Goucher living in this City has applied to me for information relative to the estate at Carlisle of the late Colonel Flower advertised for sale — to make a tender at any rate, I must request you to inform me what the premises taken together ought to bring — to do this I wish you to forward a Stating of the Value of the land under its natural advantages of Mill seat &c also the Value of the building belonging to the public considered relatively, and in fine what is the lowest both ought to fetch, suppose they are sold separately or together — I wish it may be done as soon as possible after the receipt of this letter, that the Opportunity may not be lost

Colonel Magaw —

S.H.

36745:
Philadelphia, April 4<sup>th</sup> 1789

By M<sup>r</sup> South I am favoured with yours of the 2<sup>d</sup> Inst — The Stores in the Magazine are is immediately valuable — for this purpose you will apply to the Officers of the Guard for the Assistance of the Men stationed at the Post, who for their Services shall receive one Shilling p day additional pay, as is usual in such cases — you will make out of the new list a purchasead of M<sup>r</sup> South Ble by General Irvine, for shipping such as is in the best condition, and inform me how many men will be necessary, which shall be provided — The new Muskets must be Oiled, the Ammunition also will be repot & that Service — If the articles desired to be sold will command other things in Stock & labour, the buildings, when the Stores are repaired should be kept locked, without loss of time, it is absolutely necessary for their preservation — I have commanded the Militia from your Return that you are to put, since I hope will command them in decent value, tho I am certain will not, yet as many as wanting for the preservation of the Stores intended to be held, and can only at present be classed in this way, you must do the best you can with them — From the Money arising from the Sales you will make the collection of the Sum as mentioned, and you will attend to provide with any suitable Person for paying his reasonable expences while thus employed — you will make an weekly Return, but of the Store on hand, and any that may & sold, and the expenditures of the Money received, as you have a list to ascertain, I can have no objection to your appropriating a reasonable part of any

Phil<sup>a</sup>. [strikethrough: April] — May 7 1784

Sir

I beg your attention to the letter from M<sup>r</sup> Henry, which your Excellency put into my hands yesterday. I am unhappy that the present opinion of a member of Congress is not a sufficient authority by which my conduct can be supported. —

Congress by their Resolutions of January the 8 & July [undecipherable] 1783 have directed me to allow no claims which may be brought for half pay or commutation by any officer or any class or denomination of Officers to whom it has not been theretofore been reported by promises" —

Your Excellency will perceive by the resolutions of Congress, that there is no general provision for the half pay to the Army, but what limits its extension to those [undecipherable] who shall at the same time only continue in the service to the end of the war, and that the Resolutions under which half pay related has made no provision of this nature.

I am sensible of the justice of M<sup>r</sup> Henry's case, that the same principles which granted it to those Officers who related under the resolution of Oct 1780 ought to extend to be much more to those who continue in our service a year longer, and which, will undoubtedly, on [the] floor Congress (when it shall be laid before them) do not one to allow it, but [undecipherable: warranted] in my conduct by the above mentioned [undecipherable: notice], & as I cannot find that [undecipherable] pay comes under any promises of Congress, I am truly sorry

Dear General          Philadelphia 2<sup>d</sup> May 1784

Previous to the receipt of your last letter I had agreed with a Man to serve you in the capacity of Miller, he is on board Cap<sup>t</sup>. Lane in a Brig bound for Boston and will probably leave this place to morrow the Mill Stones with their whole Iron apparatus come with him. I was induced to have the Stones made and fitted here for the twofold reason, that they could be done much better and cheaper than with you I hope you will approve what I have done the Stones are large four and half feet, bigger could not be had, as this is the largest size ever imported, and none can be furnished that are making here for want of the usual cement — The masters say where larger can be used two pair should be set up — I have drawn a Bill on you pay able three days after sight for 360 dollars
have

36804:
United States in Congress assembled

June 3. 1784

Whereas it is necessary to capitulate & holding treaties with the Indian Nations which it appears cannot be done but under the protection of an army of force. Therefore

Resolved That the Secretary in this war office be and he is hereby directed to order three hundred men of the militia directed to be raisd by the act of this day to be in readiness to march tohin and to what place or places the commissioners for negotiating with the Indians or any two of them shall direct, and that the commanding officer of the said corps give safe protection to the commissioners as they or any two of them shall require, and that the said Secretary give order to the different keepers of the public stores to furnish to the order of the commissioners or any two of them, such tents, marquees and other articles as the said commissioners shall think proper.

Resolved That the Superintendent of finance be ordered for purchasing and transmitting the articles necessary for the Indian treaties according to a list thereunto annexed to such place as the commissioners appointed to negotiate with the Indians or any two of them shall direct That he furnish such additional articles not exceeding the sum of four thousand dollars as the said commissioners shall order; And that he contract with such persons as he may think proper to furnish sixty thousand rations for the Indian treaties at such places as the commissioners

or any two of them shall direct, together with such things as shall be necessary for the expenses of the said commissioners during the Negotiation.

Resolved That the commissioners for negotiating with the Indians or any two of them being a majority of those present be empowered to do the business of the Commission, and to appoint a Secretary, messenger, interpreters, store keepers and such artificers as they may find necessary and to agree with them for their pay for which they shall draw warrants on the treasury.

THE CON[undecipherable]CUT

Cha Thomson sec<sup>y</sup>.

West Point June 7, 1784 —

Sir, In consequence of being deprived my Pay and Subsistence of Major, agreeably to Commission, on the present Reformation of the Army, I beg leave to address you — at this late Hour, because it will partly be affected, by a Legislation, who had no Commission of Major, warrant appointed in lieu of me. it was well known that Congress had made my Situation in the Service very peculiar — Whether it would place, considering the combined effect of the public finances, for which I am attentive I feel much as any Man, that one more general or many other inferior Officers, should have continued to so small a number of Men, not to devise, that it was so determined the Reduction is one sufficient, and when is once considered that the same Principles however, which obtained in the Army formerly, will, and must also prevail now it is evident

36865:
[Copy]

West Point 2<sup>d</sup> July 1784

Sir,

Genl Knox having appointed me Commissary Military Stores this department, he directed me to apply to you for the pay and emoluments annexed to the Office, I beg you to inform me what they will be, and what mode I am to receive them, Having for the last 5 or 6 months received my first pittance from the Pay Master of the Corps in which I then Served. I have let the Matter Lay dormant till now, as I did not look upon my Appointment as perminant till this Arrangement took place, but being now selected from the Corps, I ought to know upon what footing I Stand, also to prevent disputes hereafter in the fulfilment of my Accounts, which I ought to have Done very year at Least —

The Casks in which the powder in this Department is contained is in very bad Order, and if the powder is to be kept there must be new Casks provided against the next Summer, as it is not possible to get them from enough for the during of it then, and they can be got much Cheaper, as the Persons who contracts for them will be able to get his Staves of a proper Length in the fall — also the Chests and Boxes in which the Arms, and Ammunition is Contained, is they much out of repairs, by reason of their being transported from one place to another So often, that they are Shattered to peeces — I have no means in my power to remedy this money is out of the question, and there is not a Single Artificer in the Garrison (Baxtons Company) either in the Ordnance department, or amongst the hands that remain in Service, I Shall use every means in my personal power to preserve all public property till you Shall please to Order in what manner the difficultys Shall be Resolved

Favour your Very
Humble Servt
William Price
C<sup>o</sup>. M<sup>y</sup>. St<sup>rs</sup>.

[Joseph Carleton Esq<sup>r</sup>
at the War Office]

36889:
New York July 4<sup>th</sup> [?]1

Dear S<sup>r</sup>

Week eyes prevented my acknowledging the receipt of your favor of the 7<sup>th</sup> ultimo. I observe what you mention respecting the pay I received while I commanded a Regiment: Most of the period on 1775 it was in Canada under the [underline: immediate] order of Lieu<sup>t</sup> thence Richmore, and the last pay I received for it was for August 1776. During the time the pay was received by the Colonels, the Company officers were inspected by the Commanding Officers, before the Regimental one was made out — This was compaired by the Brigadier with the musters and monthly returns, before the Regimental pay roll was certified to the Pay Office, or a Warrant obtained from the Commanding General, before the money could be received — the Colonel paid the Captains the amount of their respective pay rolls — This was the mode I observed, and I conceived when I paid them I had nothing more to do with the pay — for it was received on the best vouchers we could exhibit against the Public. These being first produced to the Pay Office before any money could be received. I own it strikes me as not altogether regular

36897:
[1784 July] [4343]

You must probably have been informed of being charged with Mr Patringe, and myself, to examine the encroachments of the troops on the eastern line of this State, and to endeavour to form some compromise with the Penobscot Indians respecting the lands which they now occupy — Two

You accept the commission, and if so, when will it be agreeable to you, to go upon the business. It is my wish that we might get out the business convenient of the week after next. It is agreeable to you & Mr Patringe to go at that time, I must be necessary to make some previous provision as to Stores and a Vessel. It is proposed to lend us a State Yatch. There are two here, but it is generally said they too small. Since thank you that me know as soon as possible I am your affectionate

Knox

Gen<sup>d</sup> Lincoln
36898:
Philadelphia July 10 1784

Regarding it as a circumstance altogether impossible that I should, in the smallest degree, have incurred the displeasure of my dearest friend – I presume again to address him on the belief that my last letter has miscarried

I became one of my first cares to communicate my arrival to you, my dear General, and to detail the occurrence of my voyages and stay in England – with the progress of my mercantile pursuits, all of which have hitherto favored my best expectations – Should the same flattering prospects be continued, I think it is permitted me to hope for the supreme happiness of one day demonstrating, in some degree however short of your goodness, the perfect and indelible sense of gratitude which I had impressed.

Agreeably to the plan with which you were made acquainted before I left Princeton – I went to England under the auspices of M<sup>r</sup> Morris's introduction – and was extremely favored by M<sup>r</sup> Simpson's personal friendship in London – Through these mediums I formed the necessary connections, and attained every facility whi[ch]

pleased to complete my commercial [objects] – This done, and having passed some time with my friends, all [undecipherable] fortunate and happy in finding well, embarked at London with part of my merchandise in the month of April, and arrived at Phil[adelphia] after a very speedy and agreeable passage – having made the two voyages in forty three days only – 19 out and 24 home.

Since my return, the remainder of my goods has arrived, and have commenced Merchant – My sales have been very good, and will, I hope, be continued. ——— M<sup>r</sup> Morris has proved me with the most generous friendship, without the smallest ostentation of obliging – M<sup>r</sup> Bingham's conduct towards me, while I was in England, will ever command my gratitude, affection, and respect – Colonel Wadsworth, who was likewise in London, made me the most friendly offers – and all my friends here have been very good indeed. Reflecting, as I ever shall do, my dear General, that these advantages are derived either immediately, or a little, from the friendship with which you have honored me – you may be persuaded that no change of circumstance whether fortunate or otherwise, can ever rape the loved re-

membrance of your goodness – In prosperity I will cherish it as my proudest boast – in adversity fortune, the reflection that this merited your friendship, will administer heartfelt consolation.

I hope my dear M<sup>r</sup> Lincoln and the amiable family continue to enjoy every blessing which flow from Virtue and health – They will permit one who loves and reveres them to present his best wishes, and to offer every service of which he is capable – and that they will believe those wishes to be as gratefully sincere as they are justly due.

Should my situation capacitate the execution of any commands in this quarter – I hope you will not fail to favor me with them – They will confer pleasure, and be performed with punctuality – Be pleased to address to Mayor Bolfon – as there are two others of my name in trade here

I beg leave to trouble my dear General, with my best love to D<sup>r</sup> & Mayles – my respects to Col<sup>o</sup> & Miss Crafts and their family – M<sup>r</sup> Gore – M<sup>r</sup> and M<sup>rs</sup> A Hill, and M<sup>r</sup> S. Barrett – and if it is convenient to mention my wish to Col<sup>o</sup> Crafts that the trunk &c, may be sent to me by a vessel coming to this Port – I shall write to you soon again, and

your permission, my dear General, I will take frequent opportunities of assuring you, with how much truth and affection,
I am yours
Wlackfon

Dear General     Philadelphia 19 July 1784

On the 6<sup>th</sup> instant I was honored with yours of the 6<sup>th</sup>. I am happy to hear that the several articles forwarded to your stores had Safely got to hand, but believe I have Contradicted beyond measure at finding the Men work furnished them not so very different from my expectation — [strikethrough: the] Miller as he well knows every [undecipherable] in the best way in his [undecipherable: probably locality] ably urged the getting those articles, fearing that they could not at rate be executed there. This and the advanced price [undecipherable] with you, induced me to undertake what I had no commission to execute, and which shall forever hereafter not [undecipherable] my own judgment in such cases — but repentance without restitution I am Sensible is but poor and yet your generosity [undecipherable] the matter. Your good attention [undecipherable] were provided, nothing but the [undecipherable]

freight need be lost, as they will bring the price I gave for them here at any day, and this loss I will chearfully abide by, if you will take the trouble of reshipping them to my address — [strikethrough: the] Miller has been necessarily detained on business of his own which is now Settled, he is very anxious to come on, and will I hope be with you in a few days, his Pay only commences from the time he leaves this City, and of this you shall be advised — to all appearance and from the declarations of those who know him he is a valuable Man — My chagrin will be renewed and heightened if he does not fully answer your purpose — I believe I shall not have the pleasure of seeing you until some time in September, as I am at present much en-gaged, and shall probably remain so till then, when

when I do come, no Small part of the pleasure I anticipate, will arise from the hope I entertain of seeing in health enjoying the rewards of a life well spent, and your future exertions teeming with as rich and lasting advantages to your self and family, as you just have produced to your country at large —

I have the honour to be,
with great respect,
Sir,
your Most Obedient Servant
[undecipherable: Sam/James] Hodgson

To the hon<sup>ble</sup> M<sup>r</sup> Hardy	Philad<sup>a</sup> July 23. 1784

Sir, general Butler and myself two of the Commissioners for negotiating with the Indians inform our that neither of them, being got the Original Commission which was last found, and as they, knows to whom it was sent, and as during the negotiations it is possible that the Commissioners may be separated, they think it proper & best that each Commissioner be furnished with an addition to copy. They claim therefore to pray you that the hon<sup>ble</sup> the Com<sup>e</sup> of the State pass the following order

On motion

Ord<sup>d</sup> That an exemplification of the Commission granted by the United States in Congress assembled to the Commissioners for negotiating with the Indians, and of the resolution empowering any two of them being a majority of those present to do the business of the Commission, be made out and delivered to each of the said Commissioners under the Seal of the United States.

and upon this without [undecipherable] without

This will supply the want of the original Commission, without waiting for the Signature of the President who is gone to Black point and whose return is uncertain.

With great respect I have the honor to be &

36910:
[Honorable Benjamin Lincoln Esq<sup>r</sup> favoured by M<sup>r</sup> Whithead Boston]

Philadelphia 14<sup>th</sup> July 1784

Dear General

The bearer of this letter is M<sup>r</sup> Elisha the person hired to serve you on the capacity of Steward — he agrees to contract on wines: there on being them to embark, he has received the quarter Salary of which I before advised you — Might by the same Whithead convey, you that I will be ready to make and the Vessel calculated for this trade ready to proceed on his voyage to furnish stock to keep him profitably employed — That is as present very high from 50 to 60 per Barell, but as the berigs are coming in and are very fine the most especially fine [undecipherable] from coffee Sands, common Rye from New ham tho up in from New long land from 46 to 47 per Gallon Talle &c &c — have [undecipherable] — when requested I will regularly furnish you with the current prices as they are prices: taken in every fourteen days — Wishing firm take generally

Affectionately Yours
Sam Aragam
36911:
To the Commissioners for treating with Indian Nations

Philadelphia July 16. 1784

Gentlemen

In obedience to the Orders of the Committee of the States I have the honor to enclose you a copy of a Letter from general Clark dated "the forks of Ohio 26 April 1784" and the answer of the Committee thereto

With great respect I have the honor to be Gentlemen Your most obedient and most humble Serv<sup>t</sup>.

[M<sup>r</sup> Hazard Post master general — Philadelphia July 16. 1784]

36925:
M<sup>r</sup> Blankinship

Philadelphia 6 Cl August 1784 [remainder of line undecipherable: with the making for &/payment of this period?]

Dear Sir,

I have rec<sup>d</sup> yours of the 2. I Should be much obliged if you would acknowledge the rec. of mine, though I have no doubt but that my letters go safe, and it would have given me satisfaction to have heard you rec. that of the 27 of last Month, because I sent under your cover a letter to M<sup>r</sup> Remsen in which were enclosed fifty guineas —

I find M<sup>r</sup> Kilby was ill informed respecting the Salary of the Secretary to the commission for treating with the Indians. Gen. Butler affirms me the Salary is not fixed and I am well persuaded that off. W. Campbell's letters honestly with receiving 1850 dollars per an. he will find himself greatly disappointed. I thank you for the trouble you took in making out three copies of the commission. They will be all that will be wanted as I have had three more books cut.

I am, Sir, Your, affectionately C. T.

Gazzette - 2826]
36926:
[Secretary in the War Office]

Aug 9. 1784

Sir, I enclose you a resolution of the Com<sup>t</sup> of the States of the 26 July & one of the 27 Copies of which are transmitted to the several States. I also enclose a report of a Com<sup>t</sup> with a list therein referred to on which you are to take order. I must request the favour of you to take a copy of the list for your own government & to send a copy to the Superintend<sup>t</sup> of Finance & then return me the original that it may be deposited in the Office. I am S<sup>r</sup> Y<sup>r</sup> &c

West Point 24 August 1784

S<sup>r</sup>

I received yours of 8 Instant on the 19, in answer to which, must inform You that i am far from knowing the Situation of the Powder — if the Quarter Master had furnished orders he never let me know them and I never got to do any thing, at the powder till 18 Instant, and can make but slow progress at best, and the only Copper we had is taken fick and have it not in my power to hire One. but I shall make every Possible exertion with the assistance I receive from Major Dang [sic], but am afraid it will be far from your expectations, have seen I many by the Last of Sept know what Damiing powder there is, as it is all in the Magazines on this Side of the river. But I never could get a return of what was in each Magazine so it will be impossible to make an accurate return. the all is examined, and the Season is far advanced, and you must be possible that it is a great undertaking to aer so much powder and repair every Cask before it goes into the Magazine again and it would have been impossible if it was not for the Five Hundred half Barrels which I had made this Last winter and Spring as their is many of the Casks irrepairable and must be made do Again. I will inform you as soon as Possible the State of the Pow[d-]er if not the whole, such part as I shall examine —

For Your information I have enclosed a Copy of Gen Knoggs last Orders to me, whereby you will see upon What Orders I have Can be drawn from this Garrison —

I am Sir your most
Obed<sup>t</sup> Humb<sup>le</sup> Serv<sup>t</sup>
William Price
[D. Q. M. Store]

of the 5<sup>th</sup> — In which I have informed you fully of the Situation of
the powder; it will not be in my power to furnish you with
an Accurate return till the border is examined, after which
I shall make every exertion to forward it. You will see by my
Last letter what impediments is in the way of Compleating so
Necessary a buisness, and hope it will not be imputed to any
inattention or neglect of mine, as soon as I have Ascertained
the quantity of Damaged powder which is in the Magazeens at
Fort Clinton, I will Only Count the rest, as it will be too Late to
Rise it this season, agreeable to your Known Orders to the Com-
mandt all the powder that is taken Out each day if equal-
ized, before it is put in, which makes a great deal more trou-
ble than it would be Only to put it in the Same Casks again.
So that with what men I have I Cannot take out more than
fifty Barrells each day when fair weather. —

I am Sir
Your Very Humble Serv<sup>t</sup>
William Price

[§ to.]

Having returned to this Place from Fredericksburg in hopes there would be Money or at least a letter for me by this time, but find I am mistaken by you. After writing to you by last Mail, I learnt by George Wells Esq<sup>r</sup> that Robert Morris Esq<sup>r</sup> has sent here from Gen<sup>l</sup> Instructions for Selling the Public Stores in this State. M<sup>r</sup> Wells told me he would write to M<sup>r</sup> Morris to be [undecipherable] & to inform him if the Artillery Stores were to be Sold. M<sup>r</sup> Wells told me he has not heard yet where those Species of Stores, valuable as they may be to the Continent, would be Sold where they are deposited in this State, for perhaps at the part of their value. I doubt if any one would give fifty Dollars. (Rent of a good Storehouse for them.) I had intended to have given up my charge this Month to any Person M<sup>r</sup> Wells was to appoint to receive them, although no Order has ever come to me or from any one for that purpose. But now find I must be under the Necessity of retaining them longer, to prevent my own Ruin. A M<sup>r</sup> George [undecipherable] that has brought a Suit against me for his Dragoon hire. $[undecipherable] yet he had served as an Assistant to me last Summer. Dear Sir Philad<sup>a</sup> Oct<sup>r</sup> 15. 1794 —

There is an order for you for near 3000 dollars of George Clenny Esq. Receiver for Rhode Island, granted me by the Secy for the use of my department. If you have no time to send and get a written answer, before you are returning hither, you can call them in at the Principl place. I have advised him that gives a draught for the amount you please, in order to be discounted for the whole. You will engage a suitable character to receive, and transmit it from time to time to me. I have this day drawn on M<sup>r</sup> Nourse for 500 dollars on favour of M<sup>r</sup> M<sup>c</sup>Illvaine. That he can send before you leave Boston, he will deliver to you, or otherwise make the remittance as you think best agree in consulting together. ARTICLES of a TREATY,

Concluded at FORT STANWIX, on the twenty Second day of October, one thousand Seven hundred and eighty-four, between Oliver W<sup>olcott</sup>, Richard Butler and Arthur Lee, COMMISSIONERS PLENIPOTENTIARY from the United States in Congress Assembled, on the one Part, and the SACHEMS and WARRIORS of the SIX NATIONS on the other.

The United States of America give peace to the Senecas, Mohawks, Onondagas and Cayugas, and receive them into their protection upon the following conditions.

I. The Oneidas and Tuscaroras shall be secured in the possession of the lands on which they are settled.

II. The Castles and Properties of the Six Nations shall be restored to the full extent of the lands on which they are settled, as nearly as the same can be ascertained; and the Commissioners for the United States will endeavor to have the same ascertained at as early a period as may be convenient, and the Indian Chiefs will make known their claims and boundaries. The lines from thence shall be as follows; beginning at the mouth of a creek about four miles east of Niagara, called Oyonwayea or Johnston's Landing-Place, upon the Lake named by the Indians Oswego, and by us Ontario; from thence southerly in a direction always four miles east of the carrying place, between lake Erie and lake Ontario, to the mouth of Tehoseroron or Buffalo Creek on Lake Erie; thence south to the north boundary of the State of Pennsylvania; thence east to the end of the said north boundary; thence south along the East boundary of the said State, to the river Ohio; the said line from the mouth of Oyonwayea to the Ohio shall be the western boundary of the lands of the Six Nations, so that the Six Nations shall and do yield to the United States, all claims to the country west of the said boundary, and then they shall be secured in the peaceful possession of the country on the east side of the said boundary, and be secured as aforesaid in the possession of their several towns and settlements.

III. A line shall be drawn, beginning at the mouth of a river that empties into the Hudson, eight miles above Catskill, called Aughquaga; [undecipherable] shall be the eastern boundary of the lands of the Six Nations, from the said river to the head of Tioga, so that the Six Nations shall not claim any lands eastward of the said boundary.

[Docket:] Dec 29. 1784 / from M<sup>r</sup> Thompson / [undecipherable]

Hartford December 29. 1784

Sir

I have now the Satisfaction to acquaint you, that I am in a likely Way to bring to pecuniary Punishment, one of the Knaves that were concerned in the Alteration and altering of the Pay-Master General's Certificate N° 10974 in favor of Benjamin Pay; which you will doubtless remember I brought to your Office on the 25<sup>th</sup> of November last to be examined — The Case is as follows — (One Roger Wolcot of this State, procured a Neighbour to pass the altered Note, by his Daughter (little known) to be paid in this City at Auction, by promising them a Share of what it Sold for.) on Complaint to a Magistrate, Wolcot was apprehended and examined, and upon Testimony of the Woman, it appeared so plainly to the Magistrate, that he uttered it, with fraudulent Intent, that he was ordered to Prison, unless he gave Bail for Two hundred pounds, for his Appearance for Trial before the Supreme Court, which is now Sitting; but the Court is not yet come on — he found Bail — but it is expected that the Bonds will be forfeited by his non Appearance — the Circumstances being so strong against, that he has hardly a Chance of appearing [undecipherable] Punishment, of the [undecipherable] Grand Trial — and only by neglecting to appear, the Forfeiture of the Bonds will be all the further Reparations he, or rather his Bondsmen, will be subjected to by the Laws of this State — but besides the criminal prosecutions above recited, I have commenced an Action against him for private Damages, to be tried at a County Court on January — and altho his non Appearance on the criminal Suit, may be taken as an implicit Confession of Guilt, yet he may have the Offender to make a Defence in the civil Suit, in order to mitigate the Damages — therefore my Attorney informs me, that in order to my recovering adequate Damages, it will be necessary to produce your written Deposition, taken

upon Oath before a Magistrate, or other competent Authority, setting forth the Facts and Circumstances that arose on my bringing the Note to the Office to be compared with the Record, — and that the Deposition be certified by the Magistrate, and [undecipherable] up, and directed to the adjourned County Court, to be holden at Hartford in Hartford County and State of Connecticut on the fourth Tuesday of Jan<sup>ry</sup> 1785    I must therefore intreat the Favor of you, and the other Gentlemen attending in the Office, who were present on the 25<sup>th</sup> of November last, to take the Trouble to give your Depositions jointly or severally, relative to the Subject, in the manner above described — and that you would be pleased to forward the same by the Post, under Cover to me; early enough to answer my Purpose; and as it affects the public Interest & Credit; I suppose you can get the Letter franked, but if not, don't fail to Send it without —    To refresh your Memory

I send a Copy of the Certificate you Signed the 25<sup>th</sup> November, the purport of which ought to be expressed in the Deposition and in order to subject the Offender (who is a Man of some Property and Family) to the Severer Mulct, it may be requisite to add Some Circumstances, not contained in the Certificate, which actually took place, if you remember them — namely that upon your discovering the Alteration in the Sum from 3 89/100 Dollars to 300 Dollars, and the artful Manner in which it was executed, you was greatly surprised, and very much alarmed for the Consequences; and to prevent the Note effectually from defrauding any Body you began to deface it by drawing a Line of Ink crosswise upon the Face of it — and (as it seemed to me) was unwilling to let it go out of the Office again — but upon my making myself known, and giving you the Strongest Assurance that I should make no other Use

of it than to detect the Knavery and recover my Money, you permitted me to take it away with me — and further, if you had at that Time any Thoughts about destroying the altered Note to prevent fraud and Injury, it will be best to express it in the Deposition — because every Circumstance that tends to prove any Risque I may have run, of losing my Property, or Reputation, must enhance the Damages I sue for — but far be it from me to ask that any Thing should be inserted in the Deposition, but what is strictly true — whatever Charge you may be at, I will cheerfully repay as soon as I know what it is —    In full persuasion that you will be so obliging as to comply with my present Request, in the properest and most authentic Manner and Form, and in due Season for the purpose — I am

Sir

Your most obedient
and
very humble Servant

W<sup>m</sup> Thompson

Joseph Howell Jun<sup>r</sup> Esq<sup>r</sup>
Assistant Commissioner of
Army Accounts
Philadelphia

or either of the Gentlemen
Assistants in the Office

A Treaty concluded by the Commissioners of the United States at Fort Pits in Septr — Jany 1785.

Article 1 —

Three Chiefs one from the Wyandot and two from among the Delaware Nations shall be delivered up to the Commissioners of the United States, to be by them detained like as the Prisoners white and black taken by the said Nations or any of them, shall be restored —

Article 2<sup>d</sup> — The said Nations do acknowledge themselves and all their tribes to be under the protection of the United States, and of no other Sovereign whatever —

Article 3<sup>d</sup> — The Boundary Line between the United States and the Wyandot and Delaware Nations shall begin at the River Cayahoga and continue up the said thence to the portage between that and the Tuscanyawas Branch of Maphigomen thence down the said Branch to the forks at the crossing place about [undecipherable] low mean thence [undecipherable] to that Branch of the Great Miami which runs into the Ohio at the [undecipherable] Branch at the [undecipherable] which was taken by the French in 1752 — thence along [undecipherable] to the great Miami [undecipherable] on one [undecipherable] and down the south east side of the same to its mouth —

37127:
Hartford Jan<sup>y</sup> 23. 1785

I received your favour of the 11<sup>th</sup> and most fully acknowledge yo<sup>r</sup> goodness in forwarding the requested Depositions, which came to hand this Day — but cannot be opened but before the court, which is to meet next Tuesday. Hope they will answer the purpose of recovering civil Damages from the person accused, who has avoided a Trial on the Indictment brought by the Attorney General, only by neglecting to appear at Court, when called, all the consequence of which is the Forfeiture of the Bond (or Bail) given for his appearance — and he is by the usual practice of this State exempt from all further prosecution in the criminal Line, and liable only to make pecuniary Compensation for the Value of the Note and such Damages and Costs as may be given by a Jury on a civil Trial, wherein he may appear by himself or Attorney and make as good a Defence as if he was innocent —

Think it is a Pity that so high handed an Offence should come off so cheap, and lament that it is not in my power to forward the just wishes of the Honble Judge Stear<sup>n</sup> to bring such offenders to condign punishment — But fear it will not be affected in this State, unless Congress should think proper to recommend an adequate Punishment to be instituted on all such as Counterfeit or Alter any of the national Securities, which the frequent repeated Frauds of that kind seem loudly to require —

Jos. Howell, Esq<sup>r</sup>                    W. Thompson

37145:
Charlestown P. Carolina July 9. 1793

Dear Sir

Yours of the 18<sup>th</sup> Decemb<sup>r</sup>. Ult<sup>o</sup> & 17<sup>th</sup> M<sup>r</sup> Lewis I received. He staid but one day with me having fortunately met with a passage to Savannah, since which I have not heard from him. I informed you in my former Letter that I had forwarded yours to M<sup>r</sup> Johnson at Beaufort since which I have seen him here and am sorry to tell you that you have very little probability of Recovering your Money from him, he told me he owed you no money that he had your long sons in Philadelphia that insisted he would look over his Books and if he found he had not Discharged that Debt, he would pay you him elf in the Spring as he was then going to your City. I have received from M<sup>r</sup> Wyman forty Dollars for warrant — N<sup>o</sup> 40 which I have placed in the hands of M<sup>r</sup> Alston he has wrote M<sup>r</sup> Jarrell to pay it to you as soon as he receives his Salary due the 1<sup>st</sup> Jan<sup>y</sup> ult<sup>o</sup> for which purpose he has transmitted the Accounts by this Conveyance. Present my Compliments to the Gentlemen of the Office & to M<sup>r</sup> Owen's family;

George Reid

P.S. I have heard nothing of M<sup>r</sup> Pierce except that M<sup>r</sup> Lewis in forms me

Your favours of the 25 Nov<sup>r</sup> & 29<sup>th</sup> Jan<sup>ry</sup> last with their Inclosures I have received — The Information you want concerning any settlements which have been made with the Continental troops of the line of this State can only be obtained from Joseph Hodsine, Thomas Russell & John Flannigen Esq<sup>rs</sup> who are the Auditors appointed by law to settle the Depreciation of pay due to the troops of this State in the service of the United States up to the first of Aug<sup>t</sup> 1780 — The same persons are also appointed to Liquidate the accounts of our Continental line from the first of August 1780 to the first of January 1782. To the above named gentlemen you will therefore be pleased to apply for any Information that may be wanting in any settlement you may want to make with the Continental troops of our State, and to any Information you may want which relates to the Militia or levies of this State whose accounts I am authorised to Liquidate you will please to apply to me — Inclosed is an Account of Monies advanced by our State to the Continental & Militia Officers of this State during the time they were in Captivity — I have it not in my power positively to designate which were Continental or Militia Officers & therefore have sent you the whole list, & if any Militia Officers through Ignorance should apply to you for settlement you will please to refer them to me, & if the Continental Officers or Soldiers should apply to me I shall refer them to your Office

I find myself extremely embarrassed in settling the Account of your late Father, agreeable to the Form and Charges established by — Johnson you have Charged the U. Annapolis Feb<sup>y</sup> 25. 85

Dear Sir

Be so kind as to inform me whether Officers of the invalid Corps receives the five years full Pay exclusive of the half pay allotted them for life — This Question takes its rise from the circumstance of several Wounded Officers of this line (who are considered on the Rolls in the same light with Others & deranged by the war Office Returns at the same period) now receiving half from the State on Continental account — This is a point on which I am in doubt, & which must be determined before I proceed to close the Accounts of those in that predicament — The Question in short amounts to this, are Invalid or wounded Officers to receive both Commutation and halfpay for life? Dear Sir

At M<sup>r</sup> Hamiltons request they you will inform me of any thing is done in Congress for the agents, & likewise the allowance given by your state to Major Bowen & Cap<sup>t</sup> Bratty — As we hope the period not distant when he will begin to Issue. He wishes to obtain every information that may help towards the Execution of his Duty, in a way least liable to error & Consonant to the measures of the Northern Agents. He therefore desires to know in what manner the Pennsylvania Agents acted respecting the Identity of Persons applying for Certificates as it is not probable they could have a personal Knowledge of each Individual of the Line; My opinion on that head leads me to think an Agent can be governed by the Papers only which may be presented, & that he will be justified in Delivering the Certificates to the holder of the Discharges if they are not in the hands of the Original proprietors they are accompanied with a proper transfer — If you can advise any thing on this head please to communicate it — By an advertisement in the Philad<sup>a</sup> papers it is likewise observed that the Agents endorse the Certificates in Pennsylvania — from whence does this originate, or rather is it necessary for Cap<sup>t</sup> Hamilton to do the same — Foreseeing the necessity of Papers when I should take up the Officers Accounts, I wrote you last summer

Dear Sir

In reply to yours of the 7 Inst. I have to inform you that Congress have done nothing in respect to the allowance of Agents for the several Lines of the Army altho' M<sup>r</sup> [undecipherable] has repeatedly made application to them for that purpose. The Agent of this Line proposes to apply to the Legislature for the expences they have been at, whether any compensation will be granted them is Still in [undecipherable]. The Certificates were countersigned by Order of the Executive Council, this was done in consequence of the Land Office being opened where those Certificates were taken as Specie in Payments of Lands, whether it may be necessary for the State of Maryland to adopt this plan I shall leave to their Wisacres the every humble [undecipherable: probable reading "fancy"] opinion it will answer a very good purpose for them to do it, as then every Citizen of Maryland who wishes to have the security of his own State may purchase with [undecipherable] pay, I would advise Jack to propose this matter to the proper authority, but at the same time give a Hint of the necessity of either granting him a sum of Money to begin with or to fix a Salary — The Agents of this Line before they began to [undecipherable] advertised that all Soldiers should produce their discharges on receiving of their Certificates, and that those who had transfers Should

Benjamin Hawkins Esquire

Daniel Carroll Esquire

Elizabeth Town March 16<sup>th</sup> 1785

Sir

I have only time to acquaint you by request of my father Gen<sup>l</sup> Dayton that in the settlement he made with you in Philadelphia you made a mistake in the delivery of the notes. You will find on consulting the Continental & New Jersey scales of Depreciation that there was no Depreciation allowed at the time you dated his notes by those scales when you have actually computed it about 2 for one. You would have been informed of it before this, had not General Dayton expected for a long past that Col. Ogden would go to Philad<sup>a</sup>. and among other things settle this.
